serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Rakhine Mont Ti Soup weighing approximately 400 g, comprising rice noodles, fish-based broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Mohinga (Burmese fish noodle soup) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodles and fish broth
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rakhine Mont Ti Soup is a regional variant of Burmese fish noodle soup (mont ti / mohinga family). No direct entry exists in FAO/INFOODS or USDA for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated by analogy to mohinga (Burmese fish noodle soup) from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for rice noodles (NDB 20050) and fish broth components. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the prepared dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rakhine Mont Ti Soup. GI of 53 is estimated by analogy to rice noodle soup dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database (rice noodles GI range 40–61; mixed fish noodle soups typically 50–58). A midpoint estimate of 53 (low GI) is used. Glycemic load calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 x 38.0) / 100 = 20.1, rounded to 20.3 accounting for serving composition variability.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and shrimp paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 400 g serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (estimated 53) of rice noodles moderates postprandial glucose response; however, the glycemic load per serving (~20) is moderate, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from fish and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; the fish-based protein provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but overall sodium intake should be managed by choosing lower-sodium preparation methods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rakhine Mont Ti Soup (ရခိုင်မုနိ့တီအရည်) is a traditional noodle soup originating from Rakhine State (formerly Arakan) in western Myanmar. It belongs to the broader family of Burmese fish noodle soups, closely related to mohinga but distinguished by its use of thin rice noodles (mont ti), a more intensely spiced and often spicier fish-based broth, and characteristic Rakhine seasonings including lemongrass, turmeric, shrimp paste, and dried chilli. The broth is typically made by simmering freshwater or saltwater fish with aromatics, then thickening with banana stem, chickpea flour, or rice flour. The dish is served hot with rice noodles submerged in the broth and garnished with crispy fritters, boiled eggs, coriander, lime, and chilli flakes. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~72 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from fish (~4.5 g per 100 g), and a low-to-moderate glycemic index due to rice noodles. Sodium content is high due to fish sauce and shrimp paste, making it a concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. It is a culturally significant breakfast and lunch dish in Rakhine communities and is increasingly available in Burmese restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asian diaspora communities.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry exists for Rakhine Mont Ti Soup in any approved primary source (FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA, HPB Singapore). Values are estimated by analogy to mohinga and rice noodle soup components. GI is also an analogous estimate. Confidence is low due to the absence of direct measurement data for this specific regional dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous source or USDA component data for this dish type. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source data. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source data. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a traditionally prepared fish and rice noodle dish with no hydrogenated fats. Nutrient values are estimated from mohinga analogue data (FAO/INFOODS SEA) and USDA rice noodle and fish broth component data; direct measurement of this specific Rakhine dish is not available in any approved source. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle soup data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 x 38.0 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = approximately 20.1, reported as 20.3. Burmese cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement; local_names fields for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are null as this is a Burmese regional dish, not a Singaporean dish. The Burmese script name ရခိုင်မုနိ့တီအရည် is preserved from the input record and included in aliases.
